cc|cc-1.name: Bodily harm

items.txt: any bodily injury which interferes with health or comfort
items.eg: A split lip, a black eye, a bruise that aches for days — injury that interferes with health or comfort. (Pain with no injury isn't enough — Scatchard.)

cc|cc-1.note: Scatchard: pain alone is NOT bodily harm.

cc|cc-319.name: Consent — the 5 vitiators

count.q: Consent is not free and voluntary if obtained by … how many listed means?

items.txt: force
items.eg: He holds her down — a 'yes' given under physical force isn't free.

items.txt: threat
items.eg: 'Sleep with me or I hurt your kids' — consent under threat is no consent.

items.txt: intimidation
items.eg: A standover man's menacing presence wears her down — intimidation.

items.txt: deceit
items.eg: He lies that he's a doctor performing an exam — consent obtained by deceit.

items.txt: any fraudulent means
items.eg: Any other con that procures the 'yes' — e.g. pretending in the dark to be her partner.

cc|cc-319.note: Consent = freely and voluntarily given. Child under 13 incapable (s 319(2)(c)).

cia|s-4.name: Reasonably suspects

items.txt: personally has grounds at the time … (even if subsequently found to be false or non-existent), when judged objectively, are reasonable
items.eg: You smell cannabis and see a bong on the seat — grounds that look reasonable to an outsider, even if the search later finds nothing.

cia|s-4.note: Subjective grounds, judged objectively — survives even if the grounds turn out false.

items.txt: intends the offence
items.eg: He buys a crowbar and gloves, meaning to break into the jeweller's.

items.txt: begins to put it into execution
items.eg: He's caught prising the back door — he has started, not just planned.

items.txt: act more than merely preparatory
items.eg: Casing the joint is preparation; jemmying the lock is more than merely preparatory.

items.txt: does not complete it
items.eg: An alarm scares him off before he's inside — attempt, not the full offence.

items.txt: property offences — claim of right + no intention to defraud
items.eg: You take back your own mower from a neighbour, honestly believing it's yours — claim of right, no intent to defraud, so no stealing.

cc|cc-23.name: Accident

items.txt: not criminally responsible for an event which occurs by accident
items.eg: A reflex flinch knocks a glass that cuts someone — an unwilled event. BUT a deliberate punch that kills isn't excused just because the victim had a thin skull (s 23B(3)).

cc|cc-23.note: BUT s 23B(3): death/GBH from a deliberate use of force is not excused merely because the victim had an abnormality, defect or weakness (eggshell skull), even if unforeseeable.

cc|cc-24.name: Mistake of fact

items.txt: honest and reasonable, but mistaken, belief — judged as if the facts were as believed
items.eg: He grabs an identical bag at the carousel, honestly and reasonably believing it's his — judged as if the facts were as he believed them.

cc|cc-24.note: Can be excluded by the offence (e.g. consent under s 298; ages under s 322/331).

items.txt: believes sudden or extraordinary emergency + act necessary
items.eg: A bushfire bears down; he smashes a locked gate to get clear.

items.txt: act is a reasonable response … as the person believes them to be
items.eg: Breaking the gate is a sensible response to the fire as he sees it.

items.txt: reasonable grounds for those beliefs
items.eg: There really was smoke and flame — his belief had a reasonable basis.

items.txt: voluntary intoxication: no insanity route; but for specific-intent offences it may be regarded on whether the intention existed
items.eg: Being voluntarily drunk is no defence; but for a specific-intent crime the jury may weigh whether he actually formed the intent.

items.txt: under 10 — absolute
items.eg: A 9-year-old simply can't be charged — full stop.

items.txt: under 14 — unless proved capacity to know he ought not to do the act (doli incapax)
items.eg: An 11-year-old can be, but only if the State proves he knew he ought not to do it.

items.txt: deprived of the power of self-control
items.eg: An insult makes him lose control.

items.txt: on the sudden
items.eg: He swings then and there — on the sudden.

items.txt: before passion cools
items.eg: Immediate, before he's had time to cool off — not a return blow an hour later.

items.txt: force not disproportionate
items.eg: A shove for a shove — not a bat for a slap.

items.txt: not intended, and not likely, to cause death or GBH
items.eg: A reactive push, not a blow intended or likely to kill or do GBH.

items.txt: believes the act is necessary to defend self/another (incl. non-imminent)
items.eg: He's being set upon and hits back to protect himself (or someone else).

items.txt: act is a reasonable response in the circumstances as the person believes them to be
items.eg: The force he uses fits the threat as he perceives it.

cc|cc-7.name: Parties

items.txt: principals — does / enables / aids / counsels
items.eg: The one who stabs, the one who hands over the knife, the lookout, the one egging him on — all principals.

items.txt: common purpose — withdrawal defence in s 8(2)
items.eg: Two go to rob; one kills. Both can be liable if the killing was a probable consequence — withdrawal under s 8(2) can save you.

items.txt: counselled offence — mode immaterial
items.eg: You tell him to 'do the house on Smith St' — you're liable even if he does it a different way.

items.txt: accessory after the fact — spouse immunity s 10(2)
items.eg: Hiding the offender after the crime. But a spouse can't be an accessory to their partner (s 10(2)).

cc|cc-7.note: s 562 accessory penalty: life→14, else half.

items.txt: withdrew
items.eg: He pulls out before the job goes ahead.

items.txt: communicated by words or conduct
items.eg: He tells the others, clearly, that he's out — by words or conduct.

items.txt: took all reasonable steps
items.eg: He does what he reasonably can to undo his help — e.g. warns the intended victim.

items.txt: family relationship
items.eg: Offender and victim are partners or family.

items.txt: child present
items.eg: A child is present when it happens.

items.txt: RO breach
items.eg: It also breaches a restraining order.

items.txt: victim 60+
items.eg: The victim is 60 or older.

cc|cc-80.name: Racial aggravation — hosts

items.eg: A common assault driven by racial hostility.
An AOBH driven by racial hostility.

items.txt: s 317A
items.eg: An assault with intent, racially motivated.

items.txt: s 338B
items.eg: A threat, racially motivated.
Criminal damage, racially motivated. (Hostility based on religion alone is not within s 80I.)

cc|cc-80.note: Scope: racial group — hostility based on religion alone is not within s 80I.

items.txt: intends death
items.eg: He shoots aiming to kill.

items.txt: intends bodily injury likely to endanger life
items.eg: He stabs the torso meaning serious harm of a kind likely to endanger life (part-subjective/objective — Wongawol).

items.txt: death by act in prosecution of an unlawful purpose, likely to endanger human life
items.eg: During an armed robbery he fires to frighten and kills a bystander — death by an act in an unlawful purpose, likely to endanger life.

items.txt: unlawful killing not constituting murder — life
items.eg: A one-punch death with no intent to kill or to do life-endangering harm — an unlawful killing that isn't murder.

items.txt: assault → death, direct or indirect; foreseeability irrelevant
items.eg: A single punch drops the victim; he hits his head and dies. Assault that caused the death — foreseeability irrelevant; 20 yrs.

items.txt: administers stupefying/overpowering drug with intent re an indictable offence — 20 yrs
items.eg: He spikes her drink intending to commit an indictable offence while she's out of it.

cc|cc-277.name: The fork + fall-backs

count.q: Murder charge fall-backs — how many on the list?

items.txt: s 280 manslaughter
items.eg: The killing lacks the murder intents → manslaughter.

items.txt: s 281 UACD
items.eg: An assault that caused the death → UACD.

items.txt: s 283 attempt to kill (life)
items.eg: He tried to kill and failed → attempt to unlawfully kill (life).
s 284 — the next listed homicide alternative if murder doesn't fit.
s 291 — another listed alternative.

items.txt: RTA s 59
items.eg: Death from dangerous driving → Road Traffic Act s 59.

cc|cc-277.note: s 277: unlawful killing is murder or manslaughter. Duties: s 262 necessaries · s 265 dangerous act · s 266 dangerous thing (includes a source of ignition and a fire).

cc|cc-222.name: Assault — definition

count.q: Two ways to assault — how many limbs?

items.txt: strikes/touches/moves or applies force of any kind, directly or indirectly, without consent (or consent by fraud)
items.eg: He shoves, grabs or spits on someone without consent — direct or indirect force of any kind.

items.txt: bodily act/gesture attempts or threatens — with actual or apparent present ability
items.eg: He raises a fist as if to strike, with the apparent present ability to do it (Brady v Schatzel — even an unloaded gun).

cc|cc-222.note: Applies force incl. heat, light, electrical force, gas, odour. (Brady v Schatzel: unloaded gun can still assault.)

cc|cc-294.name: Act intended to cause GBH / resist arrest

items.txt: maim, disfigure, or disable
items.eg: He glasses someone meaning to maim or disfigure them.

items.txt: do some grievous bodily harm
items.eg: He swings a bar intending to do them grievous bodily harm.

items.txt: resist or prevent lawful arrest or detention
items.eg: He lashes out meaning to break free of a lawful arrest — sits above the ladder because of the intent.

cc|cc-294.note: By any of 8 act limbs (wound, strike, poison, suffocate…). 20 yrs. Sits ABOVE s 297 because of the intent.

cc|cc-304.name: Act or omission causing BH or danger

items.txt: duty omission / unlawful act → bodily harm or endangerment
items.eg: He drives a car at pedestrians, or a carer omits a duty, causing bodily harm or danger. 7 yrs; 20 with intent to harm (s 304(2)) — the school's JONES practical.

cc|cc-304.note: 7 yrs; with intent to harm 20 yrs (s 304(2)). The school’s own practical (JONES — car driven at pedestrians) charged s 304(2)(a).

items.txt: commit or facilitate a crime
items.eg: He punches a shopkeeper to make it easier to steal — assault to commit/facilitate a crime.

items.txt: do GBH
items.eg: He hits intending to do GBH.

items.txt: resist or prevent lawful arrest
items.eg: He struggles meaning to prevent a lawful arrest.

cc|cc-318.name: Serious assault — protected classes

items.txt: public officer (performing function OR on account of)
items.eg: Assaulting a police officer doing their job (or because of it).

items.txt: public-nature function
items.eg: Someone performing a public-nature function.

items.txt: aiders
items.eg: Those helping them.

items.txt: railway/ferry/PT drivers
items.eg: A bus / train / ferry driver.

items.txt: ambulance
items.eg: An ambulance officer.

items.txt: FES/SES/VMRS
items.eg: He shoves a firefighter at the scene — fire / SES / marine-rescue.

items.txt: hospital workers
items.eg: He lashes out at a nurse treating him in ED — hospital staff.

items.txt: court security
items.eg: He swings at the security officer on the court door.

items.txt: prison officers
items.eg: He assaults a prison officer on the unit. (Armed or in company 10 yrs, else 7.)

cc|cc-318.note: Penalty: armed or in company 10 yrs, else 7.

items.txt: blocking nose, mouth or both
items.eg: He clamps a hand over her nose and mouth — blocking the airway.

items.txt: applying pressure on, or to, the neck — done manually, or by using any other aid
items.eg: He grips or leans on her neck — manually or with any aid (a cord, a forearm).

items.txt: both skin layers (C (a minor) v Eisenhower) — 5 yrs
items.eg: A cut that breaks through both layers of skin (C v Eisenhower) — a graze isn't a wound. 5 yrs.

items.txt: 3+ occasions
items.eg: At least three separate acts of FV against her over the year.

items.txt: different days
items.eg: The three can't all be the one day — they must fall on different days.

items.txt: ≤10 years
items.eg: All inside a ten-year window.

items.txt: same person
items.eg: Same victim, each act from the prescribed FV list.

cc|cc-300.name: FV — the charge

items.txt: 14 yrs · charge specifies the period, not dates · already-charged acts excluded (s 300(8))
items.eg: Charged as a course of conduct — the charge names the period, not the dates; already-charged acts are excluded (s 300(8)). 14 yrs.

cc|cc-300.note: Designated family relationship incl. intimate personal relationship — betrothal/cultural/romantic, sex not required.

cc|cc-320.name: Child sex offences — the 5 acts

count.q: How many act limbs on the ladder?

items.txt: penetration
items.eg: Sexual penetration of the child.

items.txt: procure/incite to sexual behaviour
items.eg: Procuring or inciting the child to sexual behaviour.

items.txt: indecent dealing
items.eg: Indecently dealing with the child — an indecent act short of penetration.

items.txt: procure to indecent act
items.eg: Procuring the child to do an indecent act.

items.txt: indecently record
items.eg: Indecently recording the child — e.g. filming. (s 320 child <13; s 321 13–<16.)

cc|cc-320.note: s 320 (child <13): 20/20/10/10/10. s 321 (13–<16): 14 base, 20 if under care/supervision/authority, 7 if offender under 18.

cc|cc-321.name: Persistent sexual conduct

count.q: Sexual act on how many occasions?

items.txt: 3+ occasions
items.eg: A sexual act on three or more occasions.

items.txt: different days
items.eg: Spread across different days — not all in one episode.

items.txt: NO 10-year cap — that’s s 299
items.eg: No 10-year cap — that limit is s 299. 20 yrs; acts needn't be the same offence.

cc|cc-321.note: 20 yrs; acts need not be the same offence; one in WA.

items.txt: child 16+ under care, supervision or authority
items.eg: Child 16+ who is under the offender's care, supervision or authority. No defence of believing they were 18+ (s 322(7)); lawful-marriage defence (s 322(8)).

items.txt: indecent assault 5 yrs / aggravated 7
items.eg: An indecent assault — judged on the average-person test (Moloney v Mercer). 5 yrs / aggravated 7.

items.txt: 14 yrs / aggravated 20
items.eg: Sexual penetration without consent. 14 yrs / aggravated 20.

cc|cc-327.name: Sexual coercion

items.txt: compels sexual behaviour — 14 / 20
items.eg: Compelling sexual behaviour by force or threat. 14 / 20.

cc|cc-327.note: s 319(4): penetrates any person / carnal knowledge of an animal / self-penetration.

cc|cc-329.name: Relatives

items.txt: de facto child + lineal relative (incl. adoptive); child victim 20/10 tiers; adult incest 3 yrs
items.eg: Sexual offences against relatives — de facto child plus lineal relatives (incl. adoptive); child-victim tiers, adult incest 3 yrs.

cc|cc-329.note: s 330 incapable person (14/20, 7/10). s 331 ignorance of age no defence — applies to ss 320 & 329 only.

items.txt: kill / injure / endanger / harm a person
items.eg: 'I'll kill you' — a threat to kill, injure, endanger or harm a person.

items.txt: destroy / damage / endanger / harm property
items.eg: 'I'll torch your car' — a threat to property.

items.txt: take control of building / structure / conveyance by force
items.eg: 'We're taking this building' — taking control of a building/structure/conveyance by force.

items.txt: cause a detriment
items.eg: 'I'll ruin your reputation' — a threat to cause a detriment.

items.txt: distribute an intimate image
items.eg: 'I'll post the photos' — a threat to distribute an intimate image.

cc|cc-332.name: Kidnapping

items.txt: detain: take / entice
items.eg: He lures the child into the car — take / entice.

items.txt: detain: confine / detain
items.eg: He locks the victim in a room — confine / detain.

items.txt: detain: any other manner
items.eg: Any other way of detaining them.

items.txt: intent: benefit
items.eg: To get a ransom — intent to gain a benefit.

items.txt: intent: detriment
items.eg: To cause someone a detriment.

items.txt: intent: prevent / hinder lawful act
items.eg: To stop police doing their job — prevent/hinder a lawful act.

items.txt: intent: compel
items.eg: To compel an act. (Threat need not be made out loud — s 332(3).)

cc|cc-332.note: By threat or demand — s 332(4) threat: kill/injure/endanger/harm, or detained person not set free. 20 yrs; threat need not be made out loud (s 332(3)).

items.txt: unlawfully detains — 10 yrs
items.eg: He bundles someone into a back room with no demand or threat — bare unlawful detention. 10 yrs.

cc|cc-343.name: Child stealing

items.txt: intent to deprive carer of child <16 (or steal from its person) · takes/entices/detains OR receives/harbours — 20 yrs
items.eg: He takes, entices, detains, or receives/harbours a child under 16 to deprive a carer of them. 20 yrs; claim-of-right + parentage defences.

cc|cc-343.note: Claim-of-right + Artificial Conception Act parentage defences.

cc|cc-392.name: Robbery

items.txt: steal
items.eg: He steals the phone.

items.txt: immediately before / at / immediately after
items.eg: The force ties to the theft — snatch then shove past, or threaten then grab; one continuous act.

items.txt: violence used or threatened — to obtain, or to prevent/overcome resistance
items.eg: He uses or threatens violence — to get it or to overcome resistance. (Armed/pretends = life; armed = available for immediate use.)

cc|cc-392.note: Armed or pretends = life · aggravated = 20 · else 14. Armed = available for immediate use.

items.txt: in company
items.eg: Two of them do it together — in company.

items.txt: bodily harm
items.eg: The victim is bruised or cut in the struggle — bodily harm.

items.txt: threatens to kill
items.eg: 'I'll kill you' as he grabs the bag.

items.txt: victim 60+
items.eg: The victim is 60 or older.

items.txt: armed AND aggravated = life · armed OR aggravated = 14 · else 10
items.eg: Assault with intent to rob. Armed AND aggravated = life; armed OR aggravated = 14; else 10.

items.txt: severing with intent to steal. Special property = charge or lien, or right from holding possession
items.eg: He cuts a catalytic converter off a parked car to steal it — severing with intent to steal. Special property = a charge, lien, or right from holding possession.

cc|cc-371.name: Fraudulent intents

items.txt: permanently deprive owner
items.eg: Take a bike, never plan to give it back — the owner loses it for good.

items.txt: permanently deprive special-property holder
items.eg: A mechanic holds your car under a lien for unpaid repairs; you sneak it off the lot. You’ve deprived them of their lien, even though you own the car.

items.txt: pledge / security
items.eg: Take your mate’s watch and pawn it / put it up as collateral for a loan.

items.txt: part on a condition as to return
items.eg: Take someone’s ring and stake it in a poker game — you part with it on a condition you might not be able to meet.

items.txt: deal so it can’t be returned in original condition
items.eg: Take a neighbour’s bag of flour and bake it into a cake — can’t hand back flour now.

items.txt: (money) use at will, even intending to repay
items.eg: Take cash from the work till to bet on the horses, meaning to repay after you win. Intent to repay doesn’t save you.

cc|cc-371.hook: Permanent · Special · Pledge · Part-with · Perish · Pay-back — five P’s and a “special”.

cc|cc-409.name: Fraud

items.txt: obtains property
items.eg: Pay for a TV with a dud cheque and walk out with it.

items.txt: induces delivery to another
items.eg: Con a victim into transferring money to your accomplice’s account — they deliver it, just not to you.

items.txt: gains a benefit
items.eg: Lie on a resume to land a job, or fake qualifications for a discount. Benefit needn’t be cash.

items.txt: causes a detriment
items.eg: Spread a false rumour a rival’s restaurant failed health inspection, killing their trade. No gain to you needed.

items.txt: induces an act
items.eg: Trick a witness into signing a statement they had every right not to sign.

items.txt: induces abstention
items.eg: Deceive a creditor into not lodging a claim before the deadline, so they lose the right.

cc|cc-409.note: Intent to defraud, by deceit or any fraudulent means. 7 yrs (10 if 60+); >$50,000 not summary.
cc|cc-409.hook: Property (obtain / deliver-to-another) · Benefit / Detriment · Act (induce to do / induce to abstain).

cc|cc-414.name: Receiving

items.txt: Property — physical thing, or anything it was converted/exchanged into (steal a car, sell it, buy a laptop — the laptop is caught too)
Obtained by an indictable offence — stealing, fraud, burglary etc.; includes interstate/overseas if it’d be indictable here
Received — satisfied THREE ways (need one): Possession (alone or jointly) · aided Concealing · aided Disposing
Knowing it was so obtained — knowledge, not mere suspicion (the killer element)
items.eg: Buy a “cheap” phone with the IMEI scratched off from a bloke in a carpark — the State argues you knew it was hot.

cc|cc-414.note: You needn’t have held the goods — helping stash a stolen TV or flog it online is enough. Penalty: the source-offence penalty or 14 yrs, whichever is lower; if the source can’t be pinned down → 14 yrs flat.
cc|cc-414.hook: Elements P·O·R·K (Property · Obtained-by-indictable-offence · Received · Knowing). Received 3 ways P·C·D (Possession · Concealing · Disposing).

cc|cc-400.name: Burglary aggravations

items.txt: armed / pretends
items.eg: He's carrying a weapon (or pretends to be).

items.txt: explosive
items.eg: He's carrying an explosive.

items.txt: in company
items.eg: Two of them go in together.

items.txt: bodily harm
items.eg: The occupier is hurt during the break-in — bodily harm.

items.txt: threatens to kill or injure
items.eg: 'Stay back or I'll stab you' as he's confronted inside.

items.txt: detains
items.eg: He detains someone.

items.txt: knew or ought to have known another person in the place
items.eg: He knew, or ought to have known, a person was in the place. (Enters = any part of the body, or anything in his control — s 400(2).)

cc|cc-400.note: s 400(2) enters: any part of the body or any part of anything in possession or control.

cc|cc-401.name: Burglary

items.txt: enter or BE IN, without consent, intent to commit an offence
items.eg: He climbs through a window without consent meaning to steal — or is found inside (enter OR be in), with intent to commit an offence. Agg home 20 · home 18 · else 14.

cc|cc-401.note: Agg home 20 · non-home aggravated 20 (ba) · home 18 · else 14.

cc|cc-417.name: Unlawful possession

items.txt: possession of a thing capable of being stolen, reasonably suspected stolen/unlawfully obtained — 7 yrs
items.eg: He's holding a stack of phones he can't explain, reasonably suspected stolen — unlawful possession. Defence: no reasonable grounds to suspect. 7 yrs.

cc|cc-417.note: Defence: no reasonable grounds for suspecting. s 417A: MV + reckless/dangerous 8 · aircraft 10 · public servant/employee/director 10.

items.txt: intends damage
items.eg: He smashes the window meaning to damage it.

items.txt: OR knows or believes likely — and it results
items.eg: OR he knows it's likely his act will damage it — and it does. Either route is 'wilfully'.

cc|cc-444.name: Criminal damage / arson

items.txt: wilfully + unlawfully destroys/damages — by fire = life (arson), else 10 (14 agg/racial)
items.eg: He sets fire to a shed — by fire = arson (life). Otherwise criminal damage, 10 yrs (14 aggravated/racial). Simple damage s 445 = 2 yrs.

cc|cc-444.note: s 445 simple damage 2 yrs. s 444A ignition-source duty → s 445A breach: 15 yrs. (Samuel v Stubbs: temporary derangement can be damage.)

cc|cc-68.name: Weapon + cash / drug

items.txt: weapon + cash ($3,000+) / weapon + drug — ready access simultaneously, without lawful excuse — 5 yrs
items.eg: He has a knife and $3,000 cash (or a knife and a drug) within ready reach at the same time, no lawful excuse. 5 yrs — and self-defence is NOT a lawful excuse (Hogan v Wright).

cc|cc-68.note: Defence-of-self is NOT a lawful excuse. (Hogan v Wright.)

mda|s-3.name: To supply — the 9 verbs

items.txt: deliver
items.eg: He drops the bag to the buyer — deliver.

items.txt: dispense
items.eg: Hands out doses — dispense.

items.txt: distribute
items.eg: Spreads it among several people — distribute.

items.txt: forward
items.eg: Passes it on up the chain — forward.

items.txt: furnish
items.eg: Provides it — furnish.

items.txt: make available
items.eg: Leaves it where a buyer can take it — make available.

items.txt: provide
items.eg: Simply provides it.

items.txt: return
items.eg: Hands it back to the owner — returning it is still supply.

items.txt: send
items.eg: Posts it — send.

mda|s-3.note: Returning it is supply. To cultivate: grow/sow/scatter seed, plant/nurture/tend/harvest.

mda|s-6.name: Offences

items.txt: PWISS
items.eg: He holds a stash with intent to sell or supply it — PWISS.

items.txt: manufacture / prepare
items.eg: He cooks or prepares the drug — manufacture/prepare.

items.txt: sell / supply / offer
items.eg: He sells, supplies or offers it.

items.txt: simple: possess / use
items.eg: He has a personal smoke on him — simple possess/use. (s 7 mirrors this for plants.)

mda|s-6.note: s 7 mirrors for plants. s 5 premises/utensils.

count.q: How many schedule tiers to know?

items.txt: Sch V (PWISS): meth 2.0 g · cannabis 100.0 g · Sch VI plants: 10
items.eg: Schedule V — at/over this amount the law presumes intent to sell or supply (meth 2.0 g · cannabis 100.0 g).

items.txt: Sch VII (trafficable, s 32A): meth 28.0 g · cannabis 3.0 kg
items.eg: Schedule VII — the trafficking threshold under s 32A (meth 28.0 g · cannabis 3.0 kg).

items.txt: Sch III (jurisdiction): meth 4.0 g · cannabis 500.0 g
items.eg: Schedule III — the court-of-trial / jurisdiction threshold (meth 4.0 g · cannabis 500.0 g). Verified vs the 01/05/2026 compilation.

mda|s-32a.name: Declared trafficker

count.q: Three routes to declaration — how many?

items.txt: 3rd serious conviction in 10 yrs
items.eg: His third serious drug conviction within 10 years.

items.txt: Sch VII/VIII quantity
items.eg: He's caught with a Schedule VII/VIII quantity.

items.txt: declared criminal org
items.eg: He's a member of a declared criminal organisation. (→ confiscation.)

mda|s-32a.note: → confiscation. No limitation period for indictable MDA offences (s 32).

items.txt: stop / detain / search person + vehicle (same-sex / medical searcher)
items.eg: Stop, detain and search a person and vehicle on reasonable grounds (same-sex / medical searcher).

items.txt: JP warrant, 30 days, any time
items.eg: A JP search warrant — valid 30 days, executable any time.

items.txt: seize/copy documents + require information (passcodes; answer compellable but inadmissible against them)
items.eg: Seize/copy documents and require information (incl. passcodes — answers compellable but inadmissible against them).

items.txt: hindering
items.eg: Hindering police exercising these powers.

items.txt: used
items.eg: The knife used in the stabbing — a thing used in the offence.

items.txt: obtained by
items.eg: The cash obtained by the fraud.

items.txt: committed in respect of
items.eg: The damaged door — a thing committed in respect of.

items.txt: affords evidence or rebuts alibi
items.eg: The CCTV that proves it, or rebuts his alibi. (s 16 — reasonable force.)

cia|s-128.name: Arrest — non-serious grounds

items.txt: identity / whereabouts
items.eg: You can't establish who he is or where he lives → arrest.

items.txt: continuation / repetition
items.eg: He'll keep doing it or do it again.

items.txt: evidence
items.eg: To stop him destroying evidence.

items.txt: safety
items.eg: For his or others' safety.

items.txt: attendance
items.eg: To make sure he turns up to court. (Serious offences need no such ground — reasonable suspicion is enough.)

cia|s-128.note: Serious offence (5+ yrs or life + listed RO/bail/FV) on reasonable suspicion has / is / is about to commit — no grounds needed.

items.txt: informed of offence
items.eg: He's told what offence he's been arrested for.

items.txt: cautioned before interview
items.eg: Cautioned before any interview.

items.txt: reasonable opportunity re lawyer
items.eg: Given a reasonable chance to contact a lawyer.

items.txt: interpreter if needed
items.eg: An interpreter if he needs one.

items.txt: search (s 133/135)
items.eg: To search him (s 133/135).

items.txt: investigate
items.eg: To investigate the offence.

items.txt: interview
items.eg: To interview him.

items.txt: decide whether to charge
items.eg: To decide whether to charge. (s 140 = the detention clock.)

items.txt: stolen / unlawfully obtained
items.eg: It's stolen or unlawfully obtained.

items.txt: seizable under other law
items.eg: It's seizable under another law.

items.txt: possession unlawful
items.eg: Possessing it is itself unlawful.

items.txt: forfeitable
items.eg: It's forfeitable.

items.txt: necessary (conceal/loss · evidentiary value · forensic exam · prevent further offence)
items.eg: Seizing it is necessary — to stop loss/concealment, for its evidentiary value, for forensic exam, or to prevent a further offence.

items.txt: same powers as a warrant
items.eg: With genuine consent you get the same powers as a warrant.

items.txt: written record
items.eg: You make a written record of the consent.

items.txt: advise what/why + right to refuse or withdraw
items.eg: You tell them what you want to search and why, and that they can refuse or withdraw.

items.txt: identify
items.eg: You identify yourself to the occupier.

items.txt: intend to enter
items.eg: Tell them you intend to enter.

items.txt: copy of warrant / explain authority
items.eg: Give a copy of the warrant / explain your authority.

items.txt: seek consent (unless endanger/jeopardise)
items.eg: Seek their consent — unless that would endanger anyone or jeopardise the search.

items.txt: carrying thing / victim
items.eg: The vehicle is carrying a relevant thing or a victim.

items.txt: is a thing
items.eg: The vehicle itself is a thing relevant to an offence.

items.txt: offence in it
items.eg: An offence is being or has been committed in it.

items.txt: no bigger than necessary

count.q: Four questions, every assault charge — how many?

items.txt: Did they agree? → consent — s 222
items.eg: Did they agree? → consent (s 222).

items.txt: Did they mean it? → accident s 23B · mistake s 24
items.eg: Did they mean it? → accident (s 23B) · mistake (s 24).

items.txt: Were they reacting? → s 245/246 · 247 · 25 · 248
items.eg: Were they reacting? → provocation/defence (s 245/246 · 247 · 25 · 248).

items.txt: Were they allowed? → s 257 · s 223
items.eg: Were they allowed? → lawful authority (s 257 · s 223). Run these four at every assault charge.

cc|cc-313.name: The assault ladder

items.txt: s 313 common assault
items.eg: No real injury → common assault (s 313).

items.txt: s 317 AOBH
items.eg: Actual bodily harm → AOBH (s 317).

items.txt: s 297 GBH
items.eg: Grievous bodily harm → s 297. Climb on the INJURY, not the violence; s 294 sits above it on intent.

cc|cc-313.note: Climb on the INJURY, not the violence. s 294 sits above the ladder — on intent, not injury.

items.eg: s 221BA — the definitions of intimate image.

items.eg: s 221BB — consent is occasion-, person- and manner-specific; under-16s can't consent.

items.txt: s 221BD — distribution, 3 yrs
items.eg: s 221BD — distributing an intimate image, 3 yrs.

items.txt: demand / threat intent → s 332 kidnapping

items.txt: bare unlawful detention → s 333 deprivation of liberty
items.eg: Bare unlawful detention, nothing more → deprivation of liberty (s 333).

items.txt: child under 16, from a carer → s 343 child stealing
items.eg: A child under 16 taken from a carer → child stealing (s 343).

cia|s-117.name: Confessional — exclusionary rules

items.txt: voluntariness (R v Lee — free choice to speak or be silent; involuntary = inadmissible, NO discretion)
items.eg: Was the confession a free choice to speak or stay silent? Involuntary = inadmissible, no discretion (R v Lee).

items.txt: fairness
items.eg: Was it obtained fairly?

items.txt: public policy
items.eg: Public policy. (Caution at the outset on reasonable suspicion — Sell; no pre-interview admissions then 'adopted' — Mallard.)
